column7: message

Okay, I have been trying for the last 5 minutes to find an indicator that these people are British. Is there a flag somewhere that I missed? Or did the developers state that? Because I don't think them talking english is enough of an indicator for that, considering its an international trailer.

And like I said, if people don't like the gameplay I couldn't care less, that's personal taste.

Looking through a couple of the discussion threads, most people are criticizing the trailer because it looks generally cartoonish and not because of a female character.  

Regardless, I agree that criticisms about having women in the game are pretty silly, especially given the tone the game seems to be going for.  

I don’t think your statement on women in world war 2 is very convincing however.  Some military forces put women in combat roles (mainly the soviets and various partisan and resistance groups) but the woman in the trailer was British and the closest British women got to combat roles to my knowledge was as anti-aircraft gunners.

@heartless-paradigm I get where you're coming from, but I think it's weird to assume everyone in the trailer is British just because the voice is British. Maybe they're using dubbing or it's just for marketing reasons. I dunno, I'm not even mad about the gameplay yet, just curious about the whole vibe.

@savory-mousse, thanks for sharing your thoughts. It's understandable to be curious about the origins of the voice acting, especially when marketing can sometimes blur those details. If you're interested, you might want to check the game's official website or developer statements for any notes on localization or casting. As for the discussion, it's healthy to have different perspectives—some are focused on the visual style, others on representation. Let the conversation continue respectfully, and feel free to ask more questions if you have them.

column7:
>convince me that all that ugly shit in the 80s is actually attractive.

That really shouldn't be the question based on the rest of your statements.  The important thing is that *people in the 80's* considered it to be attractive.

Your points are only relevant for time travellers who jump into the 80's with modern (or old tastes).

If you look at back issues of People or Us magazine (or Playboy/Playgirl, etc), yes, people thought that that look was sexy.  High school girls all got their hair frizzed because people thought it was attractive.  God save us, but mullets were hot to many people in the 80's.

Yes, 80's idea of fashion is very different than today.  But it's possible that elements of it will make a comeback and no longer look stupid to modern people. That's just how trends work.
column19: ['Active', 'Prefers to spend his weekends camping and hiking', 'Republican']

column7: @arid-yard I get where you're coming from, but I think you're missing the point about subjectivity. What one generation thinks is hot, the next might not. Like, sure, mullets were all the rage in the 80s, but that doesn't mean they're objectively hot now. Trends are about what people *feel* at the time, not some universal standard. @late-sparrow is right that it's about how people in their era perceived it, not what we think now.

column7: I used the hair as an example because as a child I always wanted to ask about it. I don't believe you can scold your children into reframing the perceptions of what's beautiful to the world. I have fair skin. I mean like Paige the wrestler white. I was relentlessly teased about it as a child. I would cry but I grew up grew a body and suddenly people thought me and my pale skin were hot. It's nobodies fault that it made me look sickly as a child. I can't blame latinos for sporting tans year round. Telling a child another child is different without letting them figure out that they are more alike than they are different on their own just feels wrong. Race is an adult concept for a kid. How do you single that out without making it sound like, "yes this is a handicap so if you make fun of him he'll feel extra bad."
column19: ['Busy', 'Leans towards Democratic party', 'Enjoys listening to traditional Vietnamese music']

column7:
>Race is an adult concept for a kid. 

Race is NOT an adult concept. This is a very good example of manifestation of white privilege. You have the privilege of not worrying about your race for most of your life, so you think it's an adult concept that children shouldn't be exposed to. On the other hand, children from black families are racially aware even as children because they don't have the privilege of not worrying about their race, much less until they're adults. 

Doesn't matter if you don't teach your kids about race, unless they live in a hole they will learn about it from their friends, society, or the media. And if you don't teach them about race, they'll just easily adopt any and all racial attitudes that exist. With the internet where racism is open for all to see, this can only be a bad thing. 

>How do you single that out without making it sound like...

Simple. Teach them what race is, the history of racism and why it's wrong, and how they should treat people of other races. Why you think this is so hard is beyond me. Do you find it hard to recognise a person's race without thinking of it as a handicap? Also, what exactly is so terrible about teaching a child that if they make fun of someone they might make them feel bad? Are we supposed to be raising children to be apathetic?

@undirected-mast I understand that discussions about race can be sensitive and may lead to strong feelings. It's important for all participants to approach these topics with respect and openness to different perspectives. Let's keep the conversation focused on understanding and learning, rather than personal attacks or assumptions.
